primary_diagnosis.name: Recurrent herpes labialis (HSV reactivation) — severe painful exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Herpes labialis is a common, benign, self-limiting recurrence of herpes simplex virus (usually HSV-1) that lies latent in the trigeminal (usually the maxillary/mandibular) ganglion after primary infection. Reactivation — commonly precipitated by physiological or psychological stress, sleep deprivation, sunlight, fever or immune down-regulation — produces anterograde axonal transport of virus to the vermilion border, where replication in keratinocytes causes the characteristic sequence: a prodromal localised tingling/burning, then grouped tense vesicles on an erythematous base, which rupture to shallow serous ulcers, crust, and heal without scarring over 7-10 days. The lesion is contagious via direct contact while shedding virus (until fully crusted). Oral antiviral therapy (a nucleoside analogue) started within roughly the first 48 hours — ideally during the prodrome — truncates viral replication, shortening pain and healing time; started late it adds little. The clinically important points are that this presentation is benign and clinically evident, that the serious mimics the in-person clinician was guarding against (Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme, and early facial cellulitis) are excluded on history here, and that the one time-critical complication — herpetic keratitis from autoinoculation of the eye — is prevented by hand hygiene and safety-netted.

differential.evidence_basis: A tingling/burning prodrome followed within a day by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base at the vermilion border, in someone with recurrent stress-linked episodes — the classic cold-sore pattern.

Also causes perioral pustules/erosions, but lacks a viral tingling prodrome and the tightly grouped vesicular pattern, and classically shows thick honey-coloured crust — argued against here (secondary bacterial infection remains a possible later complication).

Painful oral sores, but aphthae occur on internal non-keratinised mucosa (never the external vermilion/keratinised skin) and do not begin as grouped vesicles — location and vesicular onset argue against.

Vesicular and painful, but zoster follows a unilateral dermatomal distribution with prominent neuropathic pain and is not typically recurrent at the same small site — argued against by the localised recurrent lip pattern.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on the AI should hold a brief differential for a painful vesicular lip eruption but should not escalate — the leading diagnosis is benign and none of the alternatives here is time-critical.

differential.evidence_basis: Tingling prodrome, grouped vesicles on erythematous base at the vermilion border, recurrent stress-linked pattern, no intraoral involvement, no target lesions and no new drug all confirm a classic cold sore and exclude the serious mimics.
No target lesions elsewhere, no mucosal/ocular/genital involvement, no new precipitating medication, and no systemic prodrome — the eruption is localised to a single lip site.
The surrounding skin is not diffusely hot, indurated or spreading, and there is no fever — no evidence of an expanding bacterial soft-tissue infection at this point.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the picture is a benign clinical diagnosis; the live task is early antiviral (if within the window), a topical barrier, strict infection-control advice, and safety-netting — not further work-up.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign constellation presenting within the ~48h antiviral-efficacy window; the only ongoing risks are secondary bacterial superinfection and autoinoculation to the eye, both of which are safety-netted rather than escalated at baseline.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A change to spreading hot induration, honey crusting, ocular symptoms, target lesions or systemic illness would move this picture and is carried as safety-nets, not as a feature of this acute episode.

red_flags.description: Ocular involvement from autoinoculation — a red, painful, watering or light-sensitive eye (herpetic keratitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Herpetic keratitis is the one sight-threatening complication of a cold sore; it needs same-day ophthalmological assessment and antiviral treatment to prevent corneal scarring and vision loss.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated HSV keratitis can cause corneal ulceration, scarring and permanent visual impairment.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial superinfection / spreading facial cellulitis — expanding hot red induration, thick honey-coloured pus, or fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: A cold sore can become secondarily infected or seed a facial cellulitis; spreading induration with systemic features needs same-day assessment and antibiotics.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive facial cellulitis, which around the central face carries a risk of deeper spread.

red_flags.description: Widespread or disseminated eruption / eczema herpeticum, or HSV in an immunocompromised host — rapidly spreading vesicles, systemic illness, or a background of eczema/immunosuppression
red_flags.clinical_significance: In atopic or immunocompromised patients HSV can disseminate (eczema herpeticum), which is a dermatological emergency requiring systemic antiviral therapy and admission.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Disseminated HSV with systemic sepsis-like illness if not treated urgently.

red_flags.description: A serious drug/immune eruption mimicking the cold sore — target lesions, mucosal/ocular involvement, or onset after a new medication (Stevens-Johnson syndrome / erythema multiforme)
red_flags.clinical_significance: SJS/EM major is a life-threatening mucocutaneous emergency; it is excluded here but is the reason the in-person clinician sought a rapid look.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed SJS/EM major, which requires urgent hospital management.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a classic cold-sore exacerbation as an emergency and dispatching an urgent home visit or ED review. The source note's urgency was an in-person after-hours billing justification; the telehealth acuity is low.

Mislabelling the external vermilion-border vesicles as intraoral aphthous ulcers (or vice versa). Location — external keratinised lip border with grouped vesicles vs internal non-keratinised mucosa — distinguishes them.
Failing to offer oral antiviral early. Benefit is greatest within ~48 hours and ideally in the prodrome; delayed initiation adds little, so recognising the timeline matters.
Managing the lip but omitting hand-hygiene advice and the ocular safety-net — herpetic keratitis from autoinoculation is the one sight-threatening complication and is preventable.
Applying or advising a topical corticosteroid to the lesion, which can worsen and prolong the herpetic infection.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Close dermatological examination confirming grouped tense vesicles on an erythematous base precisely at the vermilion border, with clean surrounding skin
Intraoral examination confirming clear buccal, labial and palatal mucosa without intraoral involvement
Clinician palpation of submandibular/cervical lymph nodes
Measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
HSV-1 PCR of a swabbed vesicle to confirm the virus (not required for management of a classic presentation)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None required acutely. Manage remotely: recognise a classic recurrent cold sore; if within roughly 48 hours of the prodrome, arrange an oral antiviral; advise a simple topical barrier (petroleum jelly on a clean cotton bud), strict hand hygiene to protect the eyes, and infection-control precautions; give the ocular (ESC-001), spreading-infection (ESC-002) and disseminated/SJS (ESC-003) safety-nets and routine GP follow-up.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — the presentation is clinically characteristic. The real risk is OVER-triaging a benign cold sore to urgent in-person care, or UNDER-managing by omitting the eye/hand-hygiene safety-net.
